summary: Netflix was the platform that released the series Self Made, which was based on my nonfiction book about Madam C. J. Walker. The series starred Octavia Spencer and was set to debut on March 20, 2020. Leading up to the release, we had many events planned, but the pandemic forced us to cancel most of them. Netflix played a big role in bringing my work to a wider audience during a very challenging time.

Octavia Spencer starred in the Netflix series Self Made, which was based on my book about Madam C. J. Walker. We had a big press day with her and other stars on March 12, 2020, in New York just before everything shut down. I remember how she was careful about not shaking hands during the pandemic, doing the Black Panther Wakanda salute instead. She was a key figure in bringing the story to life and was very present during those early pandemic days.

Madam C. J. Walker is the subject of my nonfiction book, On Her Own Ground: The Life and Times of Madam C. J. Walker. Her story was adapted into the Netflix series Self Made, which premiered in March 2020. I have a deep connection to her legacy, and it was meaningful to see her life portrayed on screen during such a tumultuous time. The series helped bring her pioneering achievements to a new generation.

entity: On Her Own Ground: The Life And Times Of Madam C. J. Walker
summary: On Her Own Ground: The Life and Times of Madam C. J. Walker is the nonfiction book I wrote about her remarkable life. This book was the basis for the Netflix series Self Made, which was about to debut in March 2020. The book and series brought attention to Madam Walker’s achievements and history, and I was involved in promoting it through events and press days right before the pandemic shut everything down. It remains a significant work in my career.

I was in New York City in February and early March 2020 for events related to the Netflix series Self Made. We managed to hold two events there before the pandemic forced cancellations. On March 12, I attended a press day and was supposed to see A Soldier's Story on Broadway that evening, but that was the day Broadway shut down. New York was a focal point for the early impact of the pandemic and felt eerily quiet as things closed.

A Soldier's Story is a play starring Blair Underwood that I was supposed to see on March 12, 2020, in New York City. That night was significant because it was the day Broadway shut down due to the pandemic. Blair Underwood was also involved in the Netflix series Self Made, which connected my experiences around that time. Missing that performance was one of the first real signs of how much the pandemic was disrupting normal life.

Blair Underwood starred in the Netflix series Self Made and also in the Broadway play A Soldier's Story. I was scheduled to see him perform in that play on March 12, 2020, the day Broadway shut down because of the pandemic. His involvement in both the series and the play made that period particularly memorable for me. It was a moment when the arts and pandemic realities collided.

Broadway was where I was supposed to see A Soldier's Story on March 12, 2020, just as the pandemic forced theaters to shut down. That night marked the beginning of a long closure that affected the arts community deeply. Being in New York for the Netflix Self Made press day and then seeing Broadway close was a stark reminder of how quickly life was changing. Broadway’s shutdown was one of the earliest and most visible signs of the pandemic’s impact.

Washington, D.C. is where I live and where I returned after the press day in New York in March 2020. I usually take the metro from Union Station to my home in Upper Northwest D.C. The city felt surreal as the pandemic began to shut down public life. I was still attending receptions and meetings before everything changed, and the transition to social distancing was gradual but palpable in D.C.

Union Station in Washington, D.C. is where I arrived after taking the train back from New York on March 13, 2020. It was eerie because the train was almost empty, and the station felt surreal as the pandemic was starting to shut down the country. From there, I took the metro home, noticing how quickly life was changing. Union Station became a symbol of the early pandemic quiet in the capital.

During the early days of the pandemic, I noticed how Octavia Spencer was greeting people with the Black Panther Wakanda salute instead of shaking hands. That gesture stood out to me as a way to maintain connection while respecting safety. It was a small but meaningful cultural reference that helped us navigate social interactions during uncertain times. Black Panther’s influence extended beyond the screen into real life during the pandemic.

The COVID-19 pandemic was a rolling disaster that unfolded in early 2020, affecting every aspect of life and work. I experienced it firsthand as events for the Netflix series Self Made were canceled one by one, and Broadway shut down on March 12. Traveling became eerie with empty trains and quiet cities, and social interactions changed drastically with new safety protocols. It was a time of uncertainty and rapid adjustment, both personally and professionally.

I have been a trustee at Columbia University, and it was during a trustees’ conference call in January 2020 that I first heard about COVID-19. One trustee lived in Seattle, which had a handful of cases at the time, and another was originally from China, providing early insights into the outbreak. Columbia’s medical school and epidemiologists were part of the conversation, helping us understand the potential impact. It was an early moment of awareness before the pandemic fully hit the U.S.

Seattle was one of the first U.S. cities to report COVID-19 cases in early 2020, with about a dozen cases when I heard about it on a Columbia University trustees call. The city’s experience was an early warning sign of the virus’s spread in America. A trustee living there shared updates that helped us understand the seriousness of the situation. Seattle’s outbreak was part of the initial cluster that made the pandemic real for many of us.

Indianapolis is where I grew up, and I remember hearing about the early COVID-19 cases there, which were countable—about a dozen. It was one of several isolated cities with cases early in the pandemic, including Boston and Seattle. Hearing about these cases made the threat feel more immediate and personal. Indianapolis was part of the early map of the virus’s spread in the U.S.

Boston was one of the cities involved in early COVID-19 outbreaks linked to a conference where some attendees traveled to other places like Indianapolis. This cluster was part of the early spread of the virus in the U.S. I remember hearing about it during discussions with Columbia University trustees. Boston’s experience highlighted how quickly the virus was moving through social and professional networks.

I was in New York City during the 9/11 attacks in 2001, working for ABC News and traveling back and forth weekly. I remember the day after the attacks vividly, when the trains were completely full as people tried to get home. That experience was a major disaster contrast to the eerie emptiness I saw on the train in March 2020 during the pandemic. Both events deeply affected how I viewed public life and safety in New York.

I worked for ABC News around the time of the 9/11 attacks and spent two days a week in New York City. Covering that event was intense and formative, and it shaped my understanding of crises. My experience with ABC News during 9/11 contrasts with the pandemic experience, especially regarding public transportation and city life. ABC News was a significant part of my journalism career during a historic moment.

During the pandemic, my longtime book club transitioned to meeting on Zoom, which was challenging because not everyone was tech-savvy. Many members were between 60 and 80 years old and had varying comfort levels with technology. Before Zoom, some used Skype or FaceTime to connect with family, but Zoom was new and had kinks to work out. It became essential for maintaining social connections during lockdowns.

Before the pandemic and widespread use of Zoom, some members of my book club used Skype to connect with their grandchildren or family. Skype was familiar to some but not universally used among the group. It was part of the early digital communication tools that helped bridge distances before Zoom became the main platform. Skype represented the initial step toward virtual socializing for many in my circle.

FaceTime was another technology some of my book club members used to stay in touch with family before we all moved to Zoom during the pandemic. It was more common for one-on-one video calls, especially with grandchildren. While FaceTime was familiar, it didn’t support larger group meetings like Zoom, which became necessary for our monthly book club gatherings. It was part of the gradual shift to more sophisticated virtual communication.
